perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The prevailing party in any dispute shall be reimbursed reasonable attorneys fees.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. The prevailing party in any dispute shall be reimbursed reasonable attorneys fees. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the arbitration clause to the end of the paragraph weakens its prominence and could be argued as not being clearly agreed upon, especially if other sections are emphasized more.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires that arbitration agreements be clear and unambiguous. By burying the arbitration clause at the end of a paragraph focused on governing law and attorney's fees, its enforceability is lessened because it does not stand out as a key component of the agreement. The law requires there to be an agreement to arbitrate, but if the agreement is unclear, it's not proper.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304 (2022) - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 73 - Arbitration Section 7304 - Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 7304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 7304. Court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration. (a) Compelling arbitration.-- On application to a court to compel arbitration made by a party showing an agreement described in section 7303 (relating to validity of agreement to arbitrate) and a showing that an opposing party refused to arbitrate, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. If the opposing party denies the existence of an agreement to arbitrate, the court shall proceed summarily to determine the issue so raised and shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration if it finds for the moving party. Otherwise, the application shall be denied. (b) Stay of arbitration.-- On application of a party to a court to stay an arbitration proceeding threatened or commenced the court may stay an arbitration on a showing that there is no agreement to arbitrate. When in substantial and bona fide dispute, such an issue shall be forthwith and summarily tried and determined and a stay of the arbitration proceedings shall be ordered if the court finds for the moving party. If the court finds for the opposing party, the court shall order the parties to proceed with arbitration. (c) Venue.-- If a controversy alleged to be or not to be referable to arbitration under the agreeme
perturbation.justification: The change weakens the arbitration clause by moving it to the end of the paragraph, making it less prominent. Pennsylvania law, as suggested by the Law Explanation, requires arbitration agreements to be clear and unambiguous. The placement could make it seem less like a key component, thereby undermining its enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7304) discusses court proceedings to compel or stay arbitration, indicating that the agreement to arbitrate must be evident for a court to compel arbitration. Moving the clause weakens its prominence, potentially leading a court to determine there was no clear agreement to arbitrate, thus contradicting the need for a clear agreement implicitly supported by the statute regarding compelling arbitration.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents, warrants and covenants that all information furnished by said party, or to be furnished by said Party, or to any other Party or Parties hereto is, or will be, true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party represents, warrants and covenants that all information furnished by said party, or to be furnished by said Party, or to any other Party or Parties hereto is, or will be, true, complete, correct and accurate to best of said Party's knowledge, ability and belief. However, this does not constitute a guarantee of accuracy.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'However, this does not constitute a guarantee of accuracy' after a statement warranting the truthfulness of information introduces a contradiction that undermines the warranty itself, which is a legal promise.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding breach of warranty, 13 Pa. Cons. Stat. § 2313
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law outlines the creation of express warranties by the seller to the buyer. By stating information is warranted to be true and accurate, then disclaiming a guarantee of accuracy, the contract contradicts itself. The clause initially creates an express warranty, then attempts to negate it, creating a legal conflict with the principles of warranty law.
perturbation.justification: The original text warrants the accuracy of information, creating an express warranty. The changed text attempts to negate this warranty by adding a disclaimer that it 'does not constitute a guarantee of accuracy.' This directly contradicts the initial warranty, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the reliance a party would place on the warranted information. The law explanation supports this by noting that such a disclaimer, following a warranty, creates a conflict with warranty law principles.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed and guaranteed that a monetary penalty will be paid by the person or persons engaged in or circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of circumvention by the "Parties" involved in this transaction, either directly or indirectly, it is agreed that a monetary penalty *may* be paid by the person or persons engaged in circumvention. This payment will additionally include all reasonable legal expenses incurred by the aggrieved party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be paid' to 'may be paid' introduces ambiguity and makes the penalty for circumvention discretionary rather than mandatory, weakening the enforcement of the non-circumvention clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding contract enforcement, generally. See e.g. ATACS Corp. v. Trans World Communs., Inc., 155 F.3d 659 (3d Cir. 1998)
perturbation.law_citation: ATACS Corp. v. Trans World Communs., Inc., 155 F.3d 659 (3d Cir. 1998)
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law emphasizes that contracts are enforced according to their terms. By changing 'will be paid' to 'may be paid', the clause becomes ambiguous. The court case ATACS Corp. v. Trans World Communs., Inc. shows that definiteness is important for contract enforcement, and this change removes that definiteness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: oof. Character questions arise in two fundamentally different ways. (1) Character may itself be an element of a crime, claim, or defense. A situation of this kind is commonly referred to as “character in issue.” Illustrations are: the chastity of the victim under a statute specifying her chastity as an element of the crime of seduction, or the competency of the driver in an action for negligently entrusting a motor vehicle to an incompetent driver. No problem of the general relevancy of character evidence is involved, and the present rule therefore has no provision on the subject. The only question relates to allowable methods of proof, as to which see Rule 405, immediately following. (2) Character evidence is susceptible of being used for the purpose of suggesting an inference that the person acted on the occasion in question consistently with his character. This use of character is often described as “circumstantial.” Illustrations are: evidence of a violent disposition to prove that the person was the aggressor in an affray, or evidence of honesty in disproof of a charge of theft. This circumstantial use of character evidence raises questions of relevancy as well as questions of allowable methods of proof. In most jurisdictions today, the circumstantial use of character is rejected but with important exceptions: (1) an accused may introduce pertinent evidence of good character (often misleadingly described as “putting his character in issue”), in which event the prosecution may rebut with evidence of bad character; (2) an accused may introduce pertinent evidence of the character of the victim, as in support of a claim of self-defense to a charge of homicide or consent in a case of rape, and the prosecution may introduce similar evidence in rebuttal of the character evidence, or, in a homicide case, to rebut a claim that deceased was the first aggressor, however proved; and (3) the character of a witness may be gone into as bearing on his credibility. McCormick §§155–161. This pattern is incorporated in the rule. While its basis lies more in history and experience than in logic as underlying justification can fairly be found in terms of the relative presence and absence of prejudice in the various situations. Falknor, Extrinsic Policies Affecting Admissibility, 10 Rutger, L.Rev. 574, 584 (1956); McCormick §157. In any event, the criminal rule is so deeply imbedded in our jurisprudence as to assume almost constitutional proportions and to override doubts of the basic relevancy of the evidence. The limitation to pertinent traits of character, rather than character generally, in paragraphs (1) and (2) is in accordance with the prevailing view. McCormick §158, p. 334. A similar provision in Rule 608, to which reference is made in paragraph (3), limits character evidence respecting witnesses to the trait of truthfulness or untruthfulness. The argument is made that circumstantial use of character ought to be allowed in civil cases to the same extent as in criminal cases, i.e. evidence of good (nonprejudicial) character would be admissible in the first instance, subject to rebuttal by evidence of bad character. Falknor, Extrinsic Policies Affecting Admissibility, 10 Rutgers L.Rev. 574, 581–583 (1956); Tentative Recommendation and a Study Relating to the Uniform Rules of Evidence (Art. VI. Extrinsic Policies Affecting Admissibility), Cal. Law Revision Comm'n, Rep., Rec. & Studies, 657–658 (1964). Uniform Rule 47 goes farther, in that it assumes that character evidence in general satisfies the conditions of relevancy, except as provided in Uniform Rule 48. The difficulty with expanding the use of character evidence in civil cases is set forth by the California Law Revision Commission in its ultimate rejection of Uniform Rule 47, Id ., 615: “Character evidence is of slight probative value and may be very prejudicial. It tends to distract the trier of fact from the main question of what actually happened on the particular occasion. It sub
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will be paid' to 'may be paid' transforms a mandatory penalty into a discretionary one, creating a legal contradiction under the Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction type. The Law Explanation suggests that definiteness is important for contract enforcement, citing ATACS Corp. v. Trans World Communs., Inc. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly address contract enforcement, the Perturbation Explanation notes that this change weakens the enforcement of the non-circumvention clause. Therefore, the change makes the penalty discretionary and unenforceable, thus, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree to abide by the following rules of non-circumvention and non-disclosure for a period of Two years from the effective date hereof. Such covenant and agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever. a) Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without the express written permission (not to be unreasonably withheld) of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available. b) For purposes of this Agreement, the term "associates" or "contacts" shall be defined as: in the case of a business entity its officers, directors, affiliates, subsidiaries, associated entities, and any other business entity in which the business entity owns five percent (5%) or more of the outstanding equity interest. c) The Parties will maintain complete confidentiality regarding this Agreement and all transactions occurring thereunder, each other's business, business sources and affiliates and each other's propriety knowledge and know-how, and will disclose such information only pursuant to the express written permission of the party who made such information available save where such information deemed to be in the public domain or under the order of a competent Court or Government Agency. d) This Agreement and each additional agreement concluded or written or verbal disclosure made between the Parties, shall be kept confidential and is not to be reproduced, communicated or distributed in any manner whatsoever except on a "need to know" basis to persons directly involved with the closing of any transaction contemplated between the Parties, or legal counsel of a Party. e) It is understood and agreed that by reason of this "Agreement" the "Parties" that are involved during the course of business transactions may learn from one another, or from the <PAGE> 2 principals the names, addresses, telephone numbers of lenders, agents, brokers, clients or others hereafter referred to as "Contracts" and or "Associates". f) It is understood and agreed that the "Contracts" of each party hereto are and shall be recognized as exclusive and valuable "Contracts" and that the parties will not directly or indirectly negotiate or participate in any transaction circumventing the party who first provided the "Contract".
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree to abide by the following rules of non-circumvention and non-disclosure for a period of Two years from the effective date hereof. Such covenant and agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for any reason whatsoever. a) Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner. b) For purposes of this Agreement, the term "associates" or "contacts" shall be defined as: in the case of a business entity its officers, directors, affiliates, subsidiaries, associated entities, and any other business entity in which the business entity owns five percent (5%) or more of the outstanding equity interest. c) The Parties will maintain complete confidentiality regarding this Agreement and all transactions occurring thereunder, each other's business, business sources and affiliates and each other's propriety knowledge and know-how, and will disclose such information only pursuant to the express written permission of the party who made such information available save where such information deemed to be in the public domain or under the order of a competent Court or Government Agency. d) This Agreement and each additional agreement concluded or written or verbal disclosure made between the Parties, shall be kept confidential and is not to be reproduced, communicated or distributed in any manner whatsoever except on a "need to know" basis to persons directly involved with the closing of any transaction contemplated between the Parties, or legal counsel of a Party. e) It is understood and agreed that by reason of this "Agreement" the "Parties" that are involved during the course of business transactions may learn from one another, or from the <PAGE> 2 principals the names, addresses, telephone numbers of lenders, agents, brokers, clients or others hereafter referred to as "Contracts" and or "Associates". f) It is understood and agreed that the "Contracts" of each party hereto are and shall be recognized as exclusive and valuable "Contracts" and that the parties will not directly or indirectly negotiate or participate in any transaction circumventing the party who first provided the "Contract".
perturbation.explanation: By removing "without the express written permission (not to be unreasonably withheld) of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available", the non-circumvention clause becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable, as it could be interpreted as preventing any future business with contacts introduced by the other party, regardless of circumstances or fairness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding unreasonable restraints on trade, 54 P.S. § 71 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this citation is related to trade names, the principle under Pennsylvania law is that restraints on trade must be reasonable. The deletion of the phrase allowing for business with written permission makes the clause an unreasonable restraint as it could block someone from doing business ever again with someone they were introduced to by the other party. Pennsylvania law favors reasonable constraints, and this becomes overbroad.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a provision allowing parties to conduct business with sources or contacts made known by another party with written permission, 'not to be unreasonably withheld'. The changed text removes this provision, creating a blanket prohibition. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania law favors reasonable constraints on trade. By deleting the phrase allowing business with written permission, the clause becomes an unreasonable restraint as it could block someone from doing business ever again with someone they were introduced to by the other party, making it overbroad and thus creating a legal contradiction. While there were no scraped law snippets, the rationale given in the Law Explanation is that the contract violates the principle of 'reasonable restraint' in Pennsylvania, which the modified contract now violates.
